plainMeaning: The Navkāra line 'destroyer of all sins' becomes a diamond fort-wall around you, and the line 'foremost of all blessings' becomes a fiery moat of glowing acacia-coals encircling it.
recitation: savva pāvappaṇāsaṇo, vapro vajamayo bahiḥ; maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ, khādirāṅgāra khātikā.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva < Sanskrit sarva ('all, entire'); opening word of the Navkāra cūlikā line quoted here.
Prakrit pāva (< Sanskrit pāpa, 'sin') + (p)paṇāsaṇa (< pra-√naś, 'to destroy utterly') + nominative -o; = Skt. pāpa-praṇāśana.
wordByWord.notes: This whole word is the second pada of the Navkāra's cūlikā, here pressed into service as the rampart-line.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit vapra ('mound, rampart, fortification-wall'), nominative singular vapraḥ → vapro by sandhi.
wordByWord.notes: Predicate: the sin-destroying line 'is' the outer rampart.

wordByWord.etymology: vaja (book's spelling of vajra, 'diamond/thunderbolt') + -maya ('made of'); nominative vajra-mayaḥ → vajamayo.
wordByWord.notes: Book reads વજ (vaja) for vajra throughout this stotra; preserved verbatim.
Locates the rampart on the outermost layer of the fortress.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit maṅgala (< Sanskrit maṅgala, 'auspiciousness, blessing'), genitive plural -āṇaṁ (= Skt. -ānām).
wordByWord.notes: Opens the third Navkāra cūlikā pada quoted here as the moat-line.
Connective within the quoted Navkāra line.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit savva (< Sanskrit sarva) + genitive plural -esiṁ (= Skt. sarveṣām).
khādira (of the khadira tree, Acacia catechu) + aṅgāra ('glowing coal, ember'); Skt. khadirāṅgāra.
wordByWord.notes: Khadira charcoal burns fiercely and long, making the moat impassable.

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit khātikā / khātrikā ('a dug trench, moat'), from √khan ('to dig'); cf. khāta ('dug, ditch').
wordByWord.notes: Predicate of the second line: 'is the moat of khadira-embers'.

literalTranslation: 'Destroyer of all sins' — that is the adamantine rampart outside; 'and of all auspicious things' — that is the moat of glowing khadira-coals.
idiomaticTranslation: Let the line 'destroyer of every sin' stand as a diamond-hard fort-wall ringing me on the outside; and let the line 'foremost of all blessings' blaze as a moat of glowing acacia-embers encircling that wall — so no evil can scale the rampart or cross the fire.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Having armoured the body and laid the diamond floor-slab beneath the feet in the previous verse, the reciter now throws up the perimeter defences. Two more padas of the Navkāra's cūlikā are mapped, and the mapping is strikingly apt rather than mechanical. The line savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo — 'destroyer of all sins' — becomes the vapra, the outermost adamantine rampart (vajamaya, bahiḥ): the very pada that annihilates sin is set as the wall that keeps sin from ever reaching the soul within. The line maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ — 'and of all blessings [the foremost]' — becomes the khātikā, but no ordinary water-moat: it is a trench of live khādira-embers. The poet's choice of khadira (acacia catechu) is deliberate — its charcoal is proverbial for burning hotter and longer than any other, so the moat is an impassable ring of fire. Note the inversion of register: the most auspicious of all things (maṅgala) is rendered as a fierce, scorching barrier. This is not contradiction but Jain logic — what is supremely auspicious for the protected soul is precisely what is unbearable to the malign forces outside. The two defences also pair as height and depth: the wall rises (bahiḥ), the moat is sunk (khāta, 'dug'), so an attacker meets fire below and diamond above. The verse thus completes the horizontal enclosure; only the lid overhead, supplied by the svāhā-sealed pada of the next verse, remains to close the cage.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The two lines quoted are the Navkāra's own self-praise (cūlikā): the mantra is 'destroyer of all pāpa' and 'the foremost of all maṅgalas'. The verse spatialises this doctrine. Sin-destruction (pāpa-praṇāśana) is positioned as the outer wall, which is the imaginative form of saṁvara — the stoppage of fresh karmic influx (āsrava): the rampart of the mantra simply does not let pāpa in. The fiery moat of maṅgala then performs nirjarā-like burning of whatever approaches. Thus the abstract claim that the Pañca-Parameṣṭhi salutation removes sin and confers supreme auspiciousness is recast as an active two-stage defence — block, then burn — without adding any doctrine foreign to the mantra itself.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: At this point in the visualisation the reciter, having sealed the body, draws the perimeter: silently 'reading' savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo while picturing a wall of diamond springing up all around, then maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ while picturing a glowing trench of coals at its foot. Practically it trains the mind to treat the Navkāra not as words recited but as a felt boundary — useful in moments of fear or temptation, where one mentally 'closes the wall and lights the moat'.

crossReferences.text: Pañca-Namaskāra (Navkāra) cūlikā: 'eso pañca namukkāro, savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo / maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ, paḍhamaṁ havai maṅgalaṁ' — the two middle padas are the lines quoted here.
crossReferences.source: Standard Jain liturgical text; cf. Navkāra entry, Wikipedia / jainelibrary editions

crossReferences.text: vapra ('rampart'), khātikā ('moat'), khadirāṅgāra ('acacia embers') — fortification vocabulary.
crossReferences.source: Monier-Williams & Apte Sanskrit-English Dictionaries (Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon)

sources.title: Namokar Mantra (Navkāra) — nine padas and the cūlikā lines, with translation
sources.consultedFor: Identifying 'savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo' and 'maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ' as the Navkāra cūlikā padas and confirming their standard meaning ('destroyer of all sins', 'foremost of all auspicious things').

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams; Apte)
sources.consultedFor: Root meanings of vapra (rampart), khātikā/khāta (moat, dug trench, √khan), khadira (Acacia catechu), aṅgāra (glowing coal), and vajra-maya (adamantine).

sources.title: Wisdomlib — definition of 'Khadira' (Acacia catechu) and 'Vapra'
sources.consultedFor: Confirming khadira = Acacia catechu and its ritual/combustion connotations, supporting the 'fierce, long-burning embers' reading of khādirāṅgāra.

notes: The book spells vajra as વજ (vaja); preserved verbatim in token/translit ('vajamayo') though glossed as vajra-maya. The verse quotes two cūlikā padas of the Navkāra ('savva-pāvappaṇāsaṇo' and 'maṅgalāṇaṁ ca savvesiṁ') and re-deploys them as the rampart and moat — the canonical pada order is not the recitation order here, which is expected for this nyāsa stotra. ખાતિકા is read as khātikā ('moat'); a by-form khātrikā exists but does not affect the sense. The hemistich is elliptical (each line equates a quoted Navkāra pada with a fortress element via an implied 'is'); the supplied copula follows the established v2–v6 pattern. No independent named composer is asserted (per preface: 'pūrva-sūris').
